title: Graham Cutts

infobox.value: (1884[[a]](#cite_note-4) – 7 February 1958), known as **Graham Cutts**, was a British [film director](/wiki/Film_director "Film director"), one of the leading British directors in the 1920s. His fellow director [A. V. Bramble](/wiki/A._V._Bramble "A. V. Bramble") believed that [Gainsborough Pictures](/wiki/Gainsborough_Pictures "Gainsborough Pictures") had been built on the back of his work.[[4]](#cite_note-Low-5): 167 Cutts worked with many leading figures in the UK film and stage world, including [Basil Dean](/wiki/Basil_Dean "Basil Dean"), [Alfred Hitchcock](/wiki/Alfred_Hitchcock "Alfred Hitchcock"), [Gracie Fields](/wiki/Gracie_Fields "Gracie Fields"), [Ivor Novello](/wiki/Ivor_Novello "Ivor Novello"), and [Noël Coward](/wiki/No%C3%ABl_Coward "Noël Coward").[[3]](#cite_note-BFI-3)

Some sources give the birth year as 1885.[[1]](#cite_note-Betts-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) The British Film Institute gives 1884.[[3]](#cite_note-BFI-3) The UK General Register Office shows a John Henry G. Cutts born in Brighton in 1884 Q1.

Katz, Ephraim (6 April 2001). *The Macmillan International Film Encyclopedia* (4th ed.). Macmillan. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0333906903](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0333906903 "Special:BookSources/978-0333906903").

Croall, Jonathan (8 June 2023). [*From Silent Film Idol to Superman The Life and Career of John Stuart*](https://books.google.com/books?id=PXXCEAAAQBAJ&dq=%22graham+cutts%22+-wikipedia+-hitchcock&pg=PA32). McFarland, Incorporated, Publishers. p. 32. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [9781476689548](/wiki/Special:BookSources/9781476689548 "Special:BookSources/9781476689548").

Slide, Anthony (2005). [*Silent Topics Essays on Undocumented Areas of Silent Film*](https://books.google.com/books?id=-t8vnF3OI8IC&dq=%22graham+cutts%22+-wikipedia+-hitchcock&pg=PA14). Scarecrow Press. pp. 14–15. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [9780810850163](/wiki/Special:BookSources/9780810850163 "Special:BookSources/9780810850163").

Robertson, James Crighton (1993). [*The Hidden Cinema: British Film Censorship in Action, 1913–1975*](https://archive.org/details/hiddencinemabrit0000robe/mode/2up?q=cutts). Routledge. p. 25. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [9780415090346](/wiki/Special:BookSources/9780415090346 "Special:BookSources/9780415090346").

Cherchi Usai, Paolo (25 July 2019). [*The Griffith Project, Volume 10 Films Produced 1919-1946*](https://books.google.com/books?id=dOXxDwAAQBAJ&dq=%22graham+cutts%22+-wikipedia+-hitchcock&pg=PA144). Bloomsbury Publishing. p. 144. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [9781839020001](/wiki/Special:BookSources/9781839020001 "Special:BookSources/9781839020001").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

British film director (1884–1958)

| Graham Cutts | |
| --- | --- |
| Born | John Henry Graham Cutts  1884 (1884)  [Brighton](/wiki/Brighton "Brighton"), [Sussex](/wiki/Sussex "Sussex"), [England](/wiki/England "England") |
| Died | 7 February 1958(1958-02-07) (aged 73–74)  [London](/wiki/London "London"), England |
| Other names | Jack |
| Occupation | Film director |
| Years active | 1922–1940 |

**John Henry Graham Cutts** (1884[[a]](#cite_note-4) – 7 February 1958), known as **Graham Cutts**, was a British [film director](/wiki/Film_director "Film director"), one of the leading British directors in the 1920s. His fellow director [A. V. Bramble](/wiki/A._V._Bramble "A. V. Bramble") believed that [Gainsborough Pictures](/wiki/Gainsborough_Pictures "Gainsborough Pictures") had been built on the back of his work.[[4]](#cite_note-Low-5): 167 Cutts worked with many leading figures in the UK film and stage world, including [Basil Dean](/wiki/Basil_Dean "Basil Dean"), [Alfred Hitchcock](/wiki/Alfred_Hitchcock "Alfred Hitchcock"), [Gracie Fields](/wiki/Gracie_Fields "Gracie Fields"), [Ivor Novello](/wiki/Ivor_Novello "Ivor Novello"), and [Noël Coward](/wiki/No%C3%ABl_Coward "Noël Coward").[[3]](#cite_note-BFI-3)

He started his career as a northern exhibitor [[4]](#cite_note-Low-5): 132 and moved into direction. When opportunities were limited in Britain, he filmed extensively in Europe.[[5]](#cite_note-6)

Ernest Betts describes him as "[seeming] likely to become a major talent in British films with *Woman to Woman* which he directed in 1922. Cutts had a polish, a know-how, an intimacy with worldly affairs which impressed the ordinary filmgoer … *The Rat*, *The Wonderful Lie* and *The Blackguard* which Cutts made in the 1920s showed a director who had learned much from the continental school and could match Hollywood in technical virtuosity."[[1]](#cite_note-Betts-1) Anthony Slide observes that "Both Graham Cutts and Herbert Wilcox deserve recognition as prominent British filmmakers who realized the necessity to bring over American actors (or more precisely former stars) to appear in their productions and thus assure them an American market."[[6]](#cite_note-7)

Cutts directed the sensational *[Cocaine](/wiki/Cocaine_(1922_film) "Cocaine (1922 film)")* (1922), the most controversial film of the 1920s.[[7]](#cite_note-8)

Reviewing  *[Paddy the Next Best Thing](/wiki/Paddy_the_Next_Best_Thing_(1923_film) "Paddy the Next Best Thing (1923 film)")* (1923), *[Variety](/wiki/Variety_(magazine) "Variety (magazine)")* concluded "This is one of the best British films yet made", and the *[Kinematograph Weekly](/wiki/Kinematograph_Weekly "Kinematograph Weekly")* lauded Cutts' "smooth" direction and his skill in the humorous sections.[[8]](#cite_note-9)

His daughter was actress [Patricia Cutts](/wiki/Patricia_Cutts "Patricia Cutts") (1926–1974).

## Selected filmography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Graham_Cutts&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Selected filmography")]

* *[The Wonderful Story](/wiki/The_Wonderful_Story_(1922_film) "The Wonderful Story (1922 film)")* (1922)
* *[Cocaine](/wiki/Cocaine_(1922_film) "Cocaine (1922 film)")* (1922)
* *[Flames of Passion](/wiki/Flames_of_Passion "Flames of Passion")* (1922)
* *[Woman to Woman](/wiki/Woman_to_Woman_(1923_film) "Woman to Woman (1923 film)")* (1923) with [Alfred Hitchcock](/wiki/Alfred_Hitchcock "Alfred Hitchcock") as assistant
* *[The White Shadow](/wiki/The_White_Shadow_(film) "The White Shadow (film)")* (1923) with Hitchcock as assistant
* *[Paddy the Next Best Thing](/wiki/Paddy_the_Next_Best_Thing_(1923_film) "Paddy the Next Best Thing (1923 film)")* (1923)
* *[The Prude's Fall](/wiki/The_Prude%27s_Fall "The Prude's Fall")* (1924) aka *Dangerous Virtue* with Hitchcock as assistant
* *[The Passionate Adventure](/wiki/The_Passionate_Adventure "The Passionate Adventure")* (1924) with Hitchcock as assistant
* *[The Rat](/wiki/The_Rat_(1925_film) "The Rat (1925 film)")* (1925) based on [Ivor Novello](/wiki/Ivor_Novello "Ivor Novello") play
* *[Die Prinzessin und der Geiger](/wiki/Die_Prinzessin_und_der_Geiger "Die Prinzessin und der Geiger")* (UK/Germany, 1925) UK title *[The Blackguard](/wiki/The_Blackguard "The Blackguard")* with Hitchcock as assistant
* *[The Triumph of the Rat](/wiki/The_Triumph_of_the_Rat "The Triumph of the Rat")* (1926)
* *[The Sea Urchin](/wiki/The_Sea_Urchin_(1926_film) "The Sea Urchin (1926 film)")* (1926)
* *[Chance the Idol](/wiki/Chance_the_Idol "Chance the Idol")* (1927)
* *[The Rolling Road](/wiki/The_Rolling_Road "The Rolling Road")* (1927)
* *[The Queen Was in the Parlour](/wiki/The_Queen_Was_in_the_Parlour_(film) "The Queen Was in the Parlour (film)")* (1927) based on the [Noël Coward](/wiki/No%C3%ABl_Coward "Noël Coward") play
* *[God's Clay](/wiki/God%27s_Clay_(1928_film) "God's Clay (1928 film)")* (1928)
* *[The Return of the Rat](/wiki/The_Return_of_the_Rat "The Return of the Rat")* (1929)
* *[The Sign of Four: Sherlock Holmes' Greatest Case](/wiki/The_Sign_of_Four_(1932_film) "The Sign of Four (1932 film)")* (1932)
* *[Looking on the Bright Side](/wiki/Looking_on_the_Bright_Side "Looking on the Bright Side")* (1932)
* *[The Temperance Fête](/wiki/The_Temperance_F%C3%AAte "The Temperance Fête")* (1932)
* *[Three Men in a Boat](/wiki/Three_Men_in_a_Boat_(1933_film) "Three Men in a Boat (1933 film)")* (1933)
* *[Oh, Daddy!](/wiki/Oh,_Daddy! "Oh, Daddy!")* (1935)
* *[Car of Dreams](/wiki/Car_of_Dreams "Car of Dreams")* (1935)
* *[Aren't Men Beasts!](/wiki/Aren%27t_Men_Beasts! "Aren't Men Beasts!")* (1937)
* *[Over She Goes](/wiki/Over_She_Goes "Over She Goes")* (1938)
* *[She Couldn't Say No](/wiki/She_Couldn%27t_Say_No_(1939_film) "She Couldn't Say No (1939 film)")* (1939)
* *[Just William](/wiki/Just_William_(1940_film) "Just William (1940 film)")* (1940)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Graham_Cutts&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-4)** Some sources give the birth year as 1885.[[1]](#cite_note-Betts-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) The British Film Institute gives 1884.[[3]](#cite_note-BFI-3) The UK General Register Office shows a John Henry G. Cutts born in Brighton in 1884 Q1.
